stacks.title: Pre-Seed / Bootstrap Stack
stacks.excerpt: The minimum viable outbound stack for pre-revenue founders: Apollo, Smartlead, and HubSpot Free — all in for under $140/mo.
stacks.content:
Why this stack at pre-seed

Before you've validated your ICP, the worst mistake you can make is over-investing in tooling. At pre-seed, you're running founder-led outbound to 20–50 prospects a week, iterating on messaging every two weeks, and learning whether your hypothesis about who buys is even correct. Spending $3,000/month on a full enrichment + sequencing + signal platform is capital destruction — you'll be ripping it out in six months anyway once you know who actually converts.

The Apollo + Smartlead + HubSpot Free combination gives you a functional outbound loop for roughly $137/month. Apollo handles prospecting database and basic sequencing in a single interface, which eliminates the need for a separate enrichment tool at this stage. Smartlead provides inbox rotation and deliverability infrastructure for cold email — a non-negotiable at any volume above 50 emails/day. HubSpot Free CRM stores your contacts, tracks deal stages, and costs nothing until you need automation or reporting depth.

The architecture: how these tools connect

The data flow is intentionally linear. You build a list, you send email, you log replies. That's it.

  • Apollo → Smartlead: Export prospect CSVs from Apollo (or use Apollo's native sequences for low volume) and import into Smartlead for multi-inbox cold email delivery. Apollo's sequence builder works for single-inbox senders; Smartlead is the upgrade path when you're warming multiple domains.
  • Smartlead → HubSpot: When a prospect replies positively, manually create or import the contact into HubSpot and open a deal. At this stage, manual hygiene is the correct choice — it forces you to personally qualify every lead.
  • Apollo CRM sync: Apollo has a native HubSpot integration. Use it to push contacted prospects automatically so you're not double-entering.
  • Email infrastructure: Budget $49/mo of your $137 for Google Workspace or secondary domain setup for inbox rotation. This is included in the cost estimate and is non-optional for deliverability.

What you're not buying yet, and why that's correct

You don't need Clay yet. Clay is a workflow automation and enrichment platform that becomes valuable when you have a repeatable ICP and need to personalize at scale across hundreds of records. At pre-seed, you're sending 30 emails a week and personalizing them by hand — Clay would add $149/month to automate a process you haven't validated yet. You also don't need a signal layer like RB2B (website visitor identification) because your traffic is near zero. And you definitely don't need an AI SDR like Artisan or 11x — those tools are designed to scale a motion you've already proven works.

When to upgrade to the Seed Stack

Three signals tell you it's time to move up: (1) You've closed your first 5–10 customers from outbound and you know the ICP profile that converts. (2) You're hiring your first SDR or sales hire and the founder can no longer personally manage prospecting. (3) Your email volume has hit the ceiling of a single Apollo account and you need multi-sender infrastructure or deeper personalization than manual copy-paste allows. At that point, layer in Clay for enrichment workflows and RB2B to capture inbound signal from your growing website traffic. The Seed Stack is the logical upgrade path.

Alternatives within tier

Instantly ($37/mo) is a direct Smartlead alternative with a comparable deliverability infrastructure — choose based on UI preference. Attio ($34/mo) is a stronger CRM if you need better data modeling than HubSpot Free offers, particularly for PLG or product-led workflows. Pipedrive ($14/mo) is the lowest-cost CRM option if budget is the primary constraint.

acf.icp_use_case: Pre-revenue or sub-$50K ARR. Founder-led outbound. ICP not yet validated. Goal: get to first 10 customers without burning $5K/mo on tooling.

pros_cons_tradeoffs.content: Apollo combines prospecting database and sequencer in a single tool — no separate enrichment vendor needed at low volume.
HubSpot Free CRM has no seat limit or expiry, giving you a professional deal-tracking layer at zero cost.
Smartlead's inbox rotation infrastructure means you can scale to 200+ emails/day without destroying deliverability.
Total spend stays under $140/mo — less than one day of a full-time SDR — leaving runway for product and hiring.
Apollo's data quality drops sharply outside North America and for SMB contacts below 50 employees — expect 15–25% bounce rates on certain segments.
No enrichment depth: you get email and basic firmographic data but no technographic signals, intent data, or recent hiring triggers.
HubSpot Free has no workflow automation, meaning every follow-up task, deal stage change, and meeting log is manual overhead.
No AI personalization layer — copy must be written by hand, which caps throughput at whatever the founder can personally produce.

Apollo sequences vs. Smartlead: Apollo's built-in sequencer is simpler to operate but limits you to one sending inbox. Use Apollo sequences until you hit deliverability friction, then migrate sends to Smartlead and use Apollo purely as a database.

Skipping Clay now saves $149/mo but means all list-building and personalization is manual. Accept this tradeoff until your ICP is proven — then Clay pays for itself in time savings within the first week at seed stage.

stacks.title: Seed Stack
stacks.excerpt: The first real GTM stack: Apollo, Clay, Smartlead, HubSpot Starter, and RB2B give seed-stage teams personalized outbound and early inbound signal for ~$1,200/mo.
stacks.content:
Why this stack at seed

You've raised your seed round, you know who buys (at least approximately), and you're about to hire your first 1–3 sales reps. This is the inflection point where manual founder-led outbound stops scaling and you need infrastructure. The seed stack adds two critical layers to the pre-seed foundation: enrichment automation via Clay and inbound intent signal via RB2B. Together, they turn your outbound from a manual grind into a semi-automated, personalized motion that a single SDR can operate at meaningful volume.

Clay is the central upgrade. At its Starter tier ($149/mo), Clay lets you build enrichment workflows that pull data from 50+ sources — LinkedIn, Clearbit, Apollo, news APIs — and auto-generate personalized email copy using built-in AI. What previously took a founder 90 minutes to personalize 20 emails now takes 10 minutes to configure a workflow that handles 200. RB2B (free tier) identifies individual visitors to your website and pushes their LinkedIn profiles to Slack — giving your team a warm outreach signal that cold outbound can't replicate. At this stage, you're spending ~$1,200/month, which is well within a seed-stage budget and delivers a stack that can support $500K–$2M ARR before it starts to creak.

The architecture: how these tools connect

The seed stack introduces a proper data layer between your database and your sequences. Data flows from source to enrichment to CRM to outbound — with intent signal entering from the inbound side.

  • Apollo → Clay: Pull prospect lists from Apollo by ICP criteria (title, company size, industry, technology). Export to Clay via CSV or direct API connector. Clay enriches each record with LinkedIn activity, recent news, job postings, and technographic signals.
  • Clay → Smartlead: Clay's AI writer generates personalized first lines or full email copy per prospect. Push enriched, personalized records to Smartlead campaigns via Clay's native Smartlead integration.
  • Smartlead → HubSpot Starter: Use Smartlead's HubSpot sync to automatically create contacts and log email activity. HubSpot Starter ($15/seat/mo) adds basic workflow automation — auto-assign leads to reps, set task reminders on reply, move deals on stage triggers.
  • RB2B → Slack → Rep queue: RB2B identifies website visitors and pushes LinkedIn profile links to a dedicated Slack channel. Reps monitor this channel for warm accounts to prioritize in their outbound sequences.
  • Clay credits budget: Clay Starter includes a credit allowance for enrichment API calls. At seed-stage volume (200–500 contacts/week), you'll likely need to purchase additional credits ($50–100/mo). Factor this into your $1,200 estimate.

What you're not buying yet, and why that's correct

You don't need an AI SDR like Artisan ($1,500/mo) or 11x ($5,000/mo) until your human reps have refined a sequence that reliably converts. AI SDRs amplify a working motion — they can't define one. You also don't need Gong ($1,400+/mo) for call intelligence until you have enough rep volume to warrant systematic coaching. And you don't need 6sense or Warmly for account-level intent data — RB2B's free-tier person-level identification is sufficient signal for a team of 1–3 reps. The signal-to-noise ratio from enterprise intent platforms doesn't justify the cost until you're running named-account programs.

When to upgrade to the Series A Stack

Four signals tell you it's time: (1) You've crossed $1M ARR and are hiring your 4th–5th sales rep. (2) Your Clay credit costs are climbing past $300/mo because volume is outgrowing the Starter tier. (3) Inbound is becoming a meaningful channel (10%+ of pipeline) and you need a proper routing and enrichment workflow for inbound leads, not just outbound. (4) A GTM Engineer or RevOps hire is joining who can manage more complex orchestration (n8n, multi-step Clay tables, Slack alerting). The Series A Stack adds Clay at full tier, Smartlead Pro, HubSpot Pro, and an AI SDR layer to handle the volume jump.

Alternatives within tier

Lemlist ($59/mo) competes with Smartlead and adds LinkedIn step sequencing natively — worth considering if LinkedIn outreach is core to your motion. HeyReach ($79/mo) is the strongest LinkedIn automation alternative for seed-stage teams doing heavy social selling. Attio is worth evaluating over HubSpot Starter if your team values modern data modeling and doesn't need HubSpot's marketing features.

acf.icp_use_case: Seed-funded B2B SaaS, 1–3 sales reps, validated ICP, $0–$2M ARR. Motion is primarily outbound with emerging inbound signal. Goal: build a personalized outbound machine that a small team can operate without a RevOps hire.

pros_cons_tradeoffs.content: Clay's enrichment + AI writer collapses the time to build a personalized outbound sequence from hours to minutes per batch.
RB2B's free tier gives you person-level website visitor identification with zero incremental cost — warm outreach signal most seed teams ignore.
HubSpot Starter's native integrations with Apollo and Smartlead mean you can build a functional CRM sync without a developer or dedicated ops hire.
The full stack still comes in under $1,200/mo — manageable for a seed-stage company with $1–3M in the bank.
Clay Starter credits run out quickly at scale — teams hitting 500+ enriched contacts/week will regularly exceed the included allotment and pay overage fees.
No AI SDR: all sequences still require a human rep to review, approve, and manage replies. Volume ceiling is roughly 150–250 touchpoints per rep per week.
RB2B free tier identifies individual LinkedIn profiles but provides no account-level buying signal, no multi-touch journey data, and no CRM sync — a meaningful gap as inbound grows.
HubSpot Starter lacks advanced reporting, custom objects, and multi-touch attribution — which become pain points above $500K ARR when leadership starts asking for pipeline analytics.
Clay Starter vs. upgrading to Clay's Explorer tier ($349/mo): Stay on Starter until your weekly enriched volume consistently exceeds 300 contacts. The credit math typically tips at that threshold.

Keeping Apollo at seed stage vs. replacing with ZoomInfo: Apollo's data is sufficient for SMB and mid-market outreach and saves $1,450/mo vs. ZoomInfo's floor. Defer ZoomInfo until you're targeting Fortune 1000 accounts where data quality gaps become pipeline-critical.

stacks.title: Series A Stack
stacks.excerpt: The full AI-native GTM engine for Series A teams: Clay, Smartlead Pro, HubSpot Pro, RB2B Pro, an AI SDR, and n8n orchestration — approximately $5,000/mo for 3–10 reps.
stacks.content:
Why this stack at Series A

Series A means you've proven the model and you're buying growth. You've got 3–10 reps, probably a GTM Engineer or early RevOps hire, and you're running outbound and inbound simultaneously. The motion is no longer founder-led or scrappy — it needs to be systematic, measurable, and defensible in a board deck. The Series A stack is where AI-native GTM stops being an experiment and becomes operational infrastructure.

The key additions over seed stage are: Clay at full tier for high-volume enrichment workflows, Artisan as an AI SDR layer that handles autonomous outbound prospecting and follow-up, n8n for workflow orchestration between tools (replacing manual handoffs), and RB2B Pro for full account-level website visitor identification with CRM sync. HubSpot Pro becomes the system of record for pipeline, marketing automation, and basic revenue reporting. The Claude API provides the AI backbone for custom copy generation, lead scoring logic, and internal tooling your GTM Engineer builds on top of n8n. Together, this stack supports $2M–$15M ARR before it starts to constrain you.

The architecture: how these tools connect

At Series A, the stack has two main loops: an outbound loop and an inbound loop, connected through HubSpot as the central CRM.

  • Outbound loop — Clay as the engine: Clay tables pull from Apollo (or ZoomInfo if you've upgraded), enrich with 10+ data sources, and use the Claude API (via Clay's HTTP connector or a custom n8n workflow) to generate hyper-personalized email copy. Enriched records with generated copy push into Smartlead Pro campaigns automatically via Clay's native integration.
  • Artisan AI SDR — parallel outbound lane: Artisan runs its own prospecting and outreach autonomously in parallel with your human reps. Configure Artisan with your ICP definition, sequence templates, and reply-handling rules. Human reps handle positive replies; Artisan manages objections and bumps. This effectively doubles your outbound capacity without headcount cost.
  • n8n orchestration layer: n8n is the connective tissue. It handles: routing RB2B signals to the right rep based on account territory, triggering Clay enrichment on HubSpot form submissions, sending Slack alerts when a prospect hits a lead score threshold, and syncing Smartlead reply data into HubSpot deal stages. Your GTM Engineer owns and maintains these workflows.
  • Inbound loop — RB2B Pro + HubSpot Pro: RB2B Pro identifies company and individual visitors, syncs to HubSpot as contacts with full session data, and triggers n8n workflows that enrich the record in Clay and route to the appropriate rep queue within 5 minutes of a visit.
  • Claude API: Used inside n8n and Clay for: generating personalized email first lines at scale, scoring inbound leads against ICP criteria, summarizing prospect research for rep briefings, and auto-drafting follow-up email suggestions based on call notes.

What you're not buying yet, and why that's correct

You don't need Salesforce yet. HubSpot Pro handles the reporting and automation complexity of a 3–10 rep team without the six-figure implementation cost or the dedicated Salesforce admin headcount you'd need to maintain it. You don't need Gong ($1,400+/mo) until you have enough rep call volume to make systematic coaching analytics defensible — most Series A teams get sufficient value from HubSpot's call recording features or a lighter tool like Chorus. And you don't need 6sense or Demandbase for enterprise ABM until you're running named-account programs with defined target account lists of 500+ companies. RB2B Pro covers the inbound signal layer sufficiently at this stage.

When to upgrade to the Enterprise Stack

Five signals indicate it's time to move: (1) You're at or past Series B with $15M+ ARR and 10+ quota-carrying reps. (2) Salesforce is being demanded by your VP of Sales, CRO, or board because enterprise buyers and partners expect it. (3) Your named-account program requires account-level intent data that RB2B Pro can't provide — you need 6sense or Demandbase's buying-stage prediction. (4) Deal review and forecast accuracy are becoming board-level concerns, requiring Gong's conversation intelligence for systematic coaching and Clari-tier forecasting. (5) Your RevOps team is spending more time fighting HubSpot's object model limitations than building pipeline. The Enterprise Stack is designed for this operating model.

Alternatives within tier

11x ($5,000/mo) is the primary alternative to Artisan as an AI SDR — 11x tends to run higher volume with more autonomous handling; Artisan has deeper CRM integration and is generally considered easier to configure for complex ICP rules. Smartlead Pro competes with Outreach at this tier if your motion is primarily email-first; Outreach becomes compelling when you need structured multi-channel cadences with phone and LinkedIn steps managed in one interface.

acf.icp_use_case: Series A B2B SaaS, 3–10 quota-carrying reps, GTM Engineer or early RevOps hire on staff, $2M–$15M ARR. Motion is outbound-led with meaningful inbound. Goal: build an AI-native GTM engine that scales to Series B without a full stack rebuild.

pros_cons_tradeoffs.content: Clay at full tier with Claude API integration enables hyper-personalized outbound at 1,000+ contacts per week — a volume that would require 3–4 additional SDR headcount without automation.
Artisan AI SDR runs parallel to human reps without seat licenses, effectively doubling outbound capacity at a fixed monthly cost rather than a variable headcount cost.

n8n as orchestration glue means your GTM Engineer can connect any two tools in the stack without a developer or costly iPaaS subscription — the $20/mo n8n Cloud entry price is the highest-ROI line item in the entire stack.
RB2B Pro's CRM sync means inbound leads are enriched and routed to the right rep within minutes of a website visit — no manual monitoring required.
Artisan requires significant configuration time upfront (ICP rules, reply handling, escalation logic) — expect 2–4 weeks before it's operating reliably without human oversight on every thread.
No enterprise CRM: HubSpot Pro's custom object limitations and reporting constraints become painful above $10M ARR, particularly for multi-product companies or complex deal structures.
No formal revenue intelligence: without Gong, call coaching is ad hoc, win/loss analysis is manual, and forecast accuracy relies on rep self-reporting into HubSpot.
Claude API costs are usage-based and can escalate quickly if your GTM Engineer builds volume-heavy workflows without rate limiting — monitor token consumption monthly.

Artisan ($1,500/mo) vs. 11x ($5,000/mo): Artisan is the correct choice at Series A unless your outbound volume target exceeds 5,000 personalized contacts per month. 11x's higher price reflects its ability to handle enterprise-scale volume and more complex autonomous reply handling.

HubSpot Pro vs. Salesforce at this stage: HubSpot Pro saves $2,000–$5,000/mo in licensing and $30,000–$80,000 in implementation cost. The tradeoff is a less flexible data model and weaker forecasting. Correct decision for most Series A teams is to delay Salesforce until the CRO demands it or you hire a dedicated RevOps engineer who can own the implementation.

stacks.title: Enterprise Stack
stacks.excerpt: The full RevOps architecture for Series B+ teams: Salesforce, ZoomInfo, Outreach, 6sense, Gong, Clay, and AI SDR — $25,000+/mo for 10+ reps with named-account programs.
stacks.content:
Why this stack at enterprise

At Series B and beyond, GTM is no longer about discovering what works — it's about scaling what works across a larger team with tighter process discipline. You have 10+ quota-carrying reps, a dedicated RevOps team, a VP of Sales or CRO with specific tooling preferences, and enterprise buyers who expect the infrastructure to match their own. The enterprise stack reflects this operational reality: every tool is best-in-class for its category, and the integration complexity is justified by the team size and deal volume that requires it.

The foundation is Salesforce as CRM, which at this stage is less a preference and more an industry expectation — for enterprise deal management, partner co-selling, revenue forecasting, and board-level reporting, Salesforce's data model and ecosystem have no practical alternative. ZoomInfo replaces Apollo as the data provider because contact accuracy at Fortune 1000 accounts is non-negotiable when a mis-targeted email to a C-suite contact has reputational cost. Outreach handles multi-channel sales engagement with the governance controls (sequence approval workflows, manager overrides, global unsubscribe management) that a 10-rep team requires. 6sense provides account-level buying intent across the entire addressable market, not just your website visitors. Gong delivers conversation intelligence for systematic coaching and forecast accuracy. Clay and 11x remain in the stack, now operating at higher volume and integrated directly into Salesforce workflows.

The architecture: how these tools connect

At enterprise scale, Salesforce is the hub that everything else connects to. The complexity of the stack is real — this requires a dedicated RevOps engineer and ideally a GTM Engineer or solutions architect to maintain.

  • 6sense → Salesforce account prioritization: 6sense's AI identifies accounts showing buying intent signals (researching your category, visiting competitor sites, hiring for relevant roles) and pushes a priority score into Salesforce Account fields. Sales leadership uses these scores to assign rep territories and set named-account lists.
  • ZoomInfo → Salesforce contacts: ZoomInfo's native Salesforce integration keeps contact and company data fresh. Sales ops runs automated enrichment jobs weekly to update titles, emails, and org structure changes across the entire contact database.
  • Clay → Outreach sequences: Clay operates as a custom enrichment and AI copy layer for the highest-priority accounts identified by 6sense. Clay workflows enrich each target contact with deep research (LinkedIn activity, press releases, earnings calls for public companies, technographic signals) and generate account-specific messaging. Enriched contacts with generated copy push into Outreach sequences via API.
  • 11x AI SDR — high-volume outbound lane: 11x runs autonomous prospecting and outreach for the mid-tier of your target account list — accounts that aren't in the top-priority named-account program but are qualified ICP. Human reps focus exclusively on the top 200 accounts; 11x handles the next 2,000.
  • Outreach → Gong → Salesforce: All rep calls are recorded in Gong automatically. Gong analyzes conversations for deal risk indicators, surfaces coaching moments to managers, and pushes engagement data back to Salesforce opportunity records. Sales leadership uses Gong's forecast dashboard alongside Salesforce's pipeline view for weekly forecast calls.
  • Claude/GPT API layer: Custom AI tooling (built by your GTM or RevOps Engineer) handles: auto-generating executive briefing documents before enterprise calls (pulling from Salesforce, LinkedIn, ZoomInfo, and 6sense data), scoring inbound enterprise inquiries for ICP fit and routing to the right team, and drafting RFP responses using a retrieval-augmented generation pattern against your product documentation.

What you're not buying that might surprise you

Even at enterprise, several tools that might seem obvious are intentionally excluded. Demandbase is not in this stack because 6sense covers the same intent data category with comparable coverage — running both creates redundant cost and conflicting account scores. Clari for revenue intelligence is worth evaluating at $50M+ ARR but is not in this baseline because Gong's forecast features and Salesforce's native forecasting cover the need for most 10–30 rep teams. Marketing automation (HubSpot Marketing Hub, Marketo, or Pardot) is a parallel track not included here — the right choice depends on whether Marketing is running demand gen programs, which requires its own stack evaluation.

When to evolve this stack

The enterprise stack doesn't have a fixed upgrade trigger — it evolves as your organization scales. Key evolution moments: (1) Clari or a dedicated revenue intelligence platform becomes necessary when forecast accuracy is a board-level concern and Gong's forecast features aren't sufficient. (2) Demandbase or a dedicated ABM platform is worth evaluating when Marketing takes ownership of named-account programs and needs a platform with campaign execution native to the intent layer. (3) A data warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery) and BI layer (Looker, Tableau) becomes necessary when your RevOps team needs custom attribution models that none of the native reporting tools can produce. These are additions to this stack, not replacements.

Alternatives within tier

Salesloft ($125/seat/mo) is the primary Outreach alternative — comparable feature set, different UX philosophy. Some enterprise teams run both for different teams (Outreach for enterprise AEs, Salesloft for SMB reps). Chorus (now ZoomInfo-bundled) is worth evaluating as a Gong alternative if you're already a ZoomInfo customer — the bundled pricing can reduce total stack cost. HockeyStack is worth evaluating for multi-touch attribution if your inbound marketing motion is significant and you need to connect marketing spend to pipeline influence in ways Salesforce's native attribution can't model.

acf.icp_use_case: Series B+ B2B SaaS or enterprise software, 10+ quota-carrying reps, dedicated RevOps team, $15M+ ARR. Running named-account programs, multi-channel sales engagement, and board-level revenue forecasting. Goal: operate a repeatable, measurable GTM engine that scales to 50+ reps without a full rebuild.

pros_cons_tradeoffs.content: Salesforce's ecosystem depth — 3,000+ native integrations, AppExchange, partner portals, and CPQ — makes it the only CRM that can serve as a true system of record for an enterprise sales organization.

6sense's buying-stage prediction across anonymous account activity gives your team a prioritized target list that no other signal source can replicate — enterprise deals are won before the first call.
Gong's conversation intelligence compounds over time: the more calls it records, the more accurate its risk flagging, coaching recommendations, and win/loss pattern analysis become.
11x at this tier handles outbound at a volume that would require 4–6 additional SDR headcount — the ROI is measurable in headcount avoided, not just efficiency gained.

Total procurement complexity: every tool in this stack requires a multi-month contract, a legal review, and a dedicated admin or integration project — budget 60–90 days to fully implement a stack change.
Salesforce requires a dedicated admin or Salesforce-certified RevOps engineer to maintain. Without one, data hygiene degrades within 60 days and the CRM becomes a liability, not an asset.

6sense and ZoomInfo pricing is negotiated, not published — your actual cost depends heavily on contract terms, seat count, and renewal leverage. Budget 20–30% above the floor estimates for realistic all-in costs.

The learning curve across this stack is significant for new reps — onboarding a new AE to full productivity takes 45–90 days when they need to master Salesforce, Outreach, Gong, and 6sense simultaneously.

Outreach vs. Salesloft: Both are enterprise-grade. Choose Outreach if your team is AE-heavy and values its governance controls and Salesforce data model fidelity. Choose Salesloft if you prioritize UI/UX and SDR-led team workflows. Most enterprise teams end up standardizing based on their VP of Sales's prior-company preference — factor that into the evaluation before you invest in implementation.

Keeping Clay at enterprise vs. replacing with ZoomInfo's built-in enrichment: Clay's flexibility to connect 50+ data sources and run custom AI workflows is not replicated by ZoomInfo's enrichment. The correct decision is to run both: ZoomInfo for bulk contact accuracy and Clay for targeted, high-value account research where depth matters more than coverage.
